items.passage:
Radio waves are a form of electromagnetic radiation occupying the longest-wavelength portion of the spectrum, stretching from a few millimeters to many kilometers. Because their energy is low, they pass through air, walls, and clouds far more easily than visible light. [■] This penetrating quality lets them carry information across great distances, which is why they became the foundation of modern wireless communication.

These signals do not all travel by the same route. [■] Near the ground, waves follow the curve of the Earth as surface currents, while higher-frequency waves reflect off the ionosphere, a layer of charged particles in the upper atmosphere. By bouncing off this layer, a single transmitter can reach receivers thousands of kilometers beyond the horizon. [■]

This long-distance reach depends on conditions that are far from stable. The ionosphere shifts with the day-night cycle and with solar activity, so a frequency that carries clearly at night may fade by afternoon. [■] Engineers respond by switching bands and adjusting power, keeping most links usable. However, as demand for wireless data climbs, even these carefully managed systems face objections that the crowded spectrum may soon offer too little room for new services.
paragraphs: Radio waves are a form of electromagnetic radiation occupying the longest-wavelength portion of the spectrum, stretching from a few millimeters to many kilometers. Because their energy is low, they pass through air, walls, and clouds far more easily than visible light. [■] This penetrating quality lets them carry information across great distances, which is why they became the foundation of modern wireless communication.

These signals do not all travel by the same route. [■] Near the ground, waves follow the curve of the Earth as surface currents, while higher-frequency waves reflect off the ionosphere, a layer of charged particles in the upper atmosphere. By bouncing off this layer, a single transmitter can reach receivers thousands of kilometers beyond the horizon. [■]

This long-distance reach depends on conditions that are far from stable. The ionosphere shifts with the day-night cycle and with solar activity, so a frequency that carries clearly at night may fade by afternoon. [■] Engineers respond by switching bands and adjusting power, keeping most links usable. However, as demand for wireless data climbs, even these carefully managed systems face objections that the crowded spectrum may soon offer too little room for new services.

questions.stem: The word "shifts" in paragraph 3 is closest in meaning to

options.A: settles
options.B: hardens
options.C: travels
options.D: changes

questions.explanation: The passage says the ionosphere "shifts with the day-night cycle," so that a frequency clear at night may fade by afternoon, indicating that it changes over time.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, how can a single transmitter reach receivers far beyond the horizon?

options.A: By following the curve of the Earth as surface currents
options.B: By raising the power of its ground-based antenna
options.C: By reflecting its waves off the ionosphere
options.D: By converting its signals into visible light

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states that "by bouncing off this layer" — the ionosphere — "a single transmitter can reach receivers thousands of kilometers beyond the horizon."

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about reliable radio communication?

options.A: It may require changing frequencies at different times of day.
options.B: It is entirely unaffected by solar activity.
options.C: It always works better at night than during the day.
options.D: It will soon become impossible as the ionosphere disappears.

questions.explanation: Because a frequency clear at night "may fade by afternoon" and engineers respond "by switching bands," reliable links imply adjusting frequencies as conditions change through the day.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention that a frequency clear at night may fade by afternoon in paragraph 3?

options.A: To prove that nighttime is the only usable period for broadcasting
options.B: To illustrate how changing atmospheric conditions affect signal reliability
options.C: To argue that engineers cannot improve radio systems at all
options.D: To describe how transmitters convert signals into waves

questions.explanation: The example follows the claim that the ionosphere is "far from stable" and shows concretely how its shifts make a given frequency unreliable over the course of a day.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. 'For example, shortwave broadcasts can travel between continents by repeatedly bouncing between the ionosphere and the ground.' Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Slot 1 [■]
options.B: Slot 2 [■]
options.C: Slot 3 [■]
options.D: Slot 4 [■]

questions.explanation: Slot 3 follows the sentence explaining that bouncing off the ionosphere lets a transmitter reach far beyond the horizon, so the shortwave example directly elaborates that mechanism.

items.passage:
Deforestation is the large-scale clearing of forests for timber, agriculture, or urban settlement. In tropical regions it proceeds fastest, stripping away trees that store carbon and shelter countless species. [■] Each year millions of hectares fall, and the carbon once locked inside the wood escapes into the atmosphere. When forests vanish, exposed soil loses moisture and nutrients, rivers silt up, and local rainfall often declines, since forests recycle much of the water that falls on them. [■]

These losses have pushed governments and communities toward restoration. Reforestation programs replant cleared ground, while agroforestry blends crops with trees so that farmers earn income without stripping the land bare. [■] Satellite mapping now guides planners to the most degraded areas, where recovery yields the greatest ecological return.

Such efforts show measurable gains, yet they cannot instantly replace what was lost. A young plantation stores far less carbon and shelters fewer species than the mature forest it succeeds, and its seedlings may take decades to grow. [■] The intricate web of soil fungi and insects that supports a forest returns even more slowly. Recognizing this gap, planners increasingly protect standing forests first and design new projects around native species suited to local soils.
paragraphs: Deforestation is the large-scale clearing of forests for timber, agriculture, or urban settlement. In tropical regions it proceeds fastest, stripping away trees that store carbon and shelter countless species. [■] Each year millions of hectares fall, and the carbon once locked inside the wood escapes into the atmosphere. When forests vanish, exposed soil loses moisture and nutrients, rivers silt up, and local rainfall often declines, since forests recycle much of the water that falls on them. [■]

These losses have pushed governments and communities toward restoration. Reforestation programs replant cleared ground, while agroforestry blends crops with trees so that farmers earn income without stripping the land bare. [■] Satellite mapping now guides planners to the most degraded areas, where recovery yields the greatest ecological return.

Such efforts show measurable gains, yet they cannot instantly replace what was lost. A young plantation stores far less carbon and shelters fewer species than the mature forest it succeeds, and its seedlings may take decades to grow. [■] The intricate web of soil fungi and insects that supports a forest returns even more slowly. Recognizing this gap, planners increasingly protect standing forests first and design new projects around native species suited to local soils.

questions.stem: The word "degraded" in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to

options.A: fertile
options.B: protected
options.C: expanded
options.D: damaged

questions.explanation: Satellite mapping guides planners to the most degraded areas "where recovery yields the greatest ecological return," so these are the most damaged, in need of repair.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, why does local rainfall often decline when forests vanish?

options.A: Because rivers silt up and overflow their banks
options.B: Because forests recycle much of the water that falls on them
options.C: Because urban settlements consume the water supply
options.D: Because stored carbon warms the surrounding atmosphere

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 states that rainfall declines "since forests recycle much of the water that falls on them," so their removal reduces that recycling.

questions.stem: According to the passage, all of the following are described as restoration approaches EXCEPT

options.A: replanting cleared ground with new trees
options.B: blending crops with trees to keep the land productive
options.C: using satellite mapping to target degraded areas
options.D: draining rivers to expose new farmland

questions.explanation: The passage lists reforestation, agroforestry, and satellite-guided planning; draining rivers to create farmland is never mentioned as a restoration method.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about newly planted forests?

options.A: They cannot immediately match the benefits of mature forests.
options.B: They store more carbon than the forests they replace.
options.C: They make native species unsuitable for restoration.
options.D: They reach full maturity within a single growing season.

questions.explanation: Because a young plantation "stores far less carbon and shelters fewer species" and its seedlings "may take decades to grow," replanting cannot instantly restore a mature forest's value.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. 'For example, in parts of Brazil, farmers grow shade coffee beneath native trees, harvesting a crop while the canopy slowly regrows.' Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Slot 1 [■]
options.B: Slot 2 [■]
options.C: Slot 3 [■]
options.D: Slot 4 [■]

questions.explanation: Slot 3 follows the description of agroforestry, which blends crops with trees, so the shade-coffee case is a concrete example of that practice.

items.passage:
A supply chain is the connected network of suppliers, factories, and transporters that moves a product from raw material to final buyer. In early commerce, these chains were short and local, since merchants sold mainly what nearby workshops could produce. [■] Goods moved slowly by cart or ship, and a single delay could leave shelves empty for weeks, so traders kept large stocks as protection against uncertainty.

These cautious methods began to strain as trade widened across oceans and continents. Holding vast inventories tied up money and warehouse space, while distant suppliers made shortages harder to predict. [■] In the twentieth century, manufacturers adopted just-in-time systems, ordering parts to arrive exactly when needed rather than storing them for months. [■]

This lean approach cut costs sharply but left firms exposed when a single disruption halted the flow of parts. [■] Modern managers now track shipments through digital sensors and predictive software, rerouting orders the moment a port closes or a storm approaches. Researchers are continuously uncovering how these vast networks absorb shocks, measuring which designs recover fastest after a sudden break.
paragraphs: A supply chain is the connected network of suppliers, factories, and transporters that moves a product from raw material to final buyer. In early commerce, these chains were short and local, since merchants sold mainly what nearby workshops could produce. [■] Goods moved slowly by cart or ship, and a single delay could leave shelves empty for weeks, so traders kept large stocks as protection against uncertainty.

These cautious methods began to strain as trade widened across oceans and continents. Holding vast inventories tied up money and warehouse space, while distant suppliers made shortages harder to predict. [■] In the twentieth century, manufacturers adopted just-in-time systems, ordering parts to arrive exactly when needed rather than storing them for months. [■]

This lean approach cut costs sharply but left firms exposed when a single disruption halted the flow of parts. [■] Modern managers now track shipments through digital sensors and predictive software, rerouting orders the moment a port closes or a storm approaches. Researchers are continuously uncovering how these vast networks absorb shocks, measuring which designs recover fastest after a sudden break.

questions.stem: The word "halted" in paragraph 3 is closest in meaning to

options.A: sped
options.B: stopped
options.C: measured
options.D: doubled

questions.explanation: Firms were "left exposed when a single disruption halted the flow of parts," so "halted" means brought the flow to a stop.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, why did early traders keep large stocks of goods?

options.A: Because nearby workshops produced far too many goods
options.B: Because customers constantly demanded greater variety
options.C: Because building extra warehouses was inexpensive
options.D: Because slow transport and delays could leave shelves empty

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 explains that goods moved slowly and "a single delay could leave shelves empty for weeks, so traders kept large stocks as protection against uncertainty."

questions.stem: What does "This lean approach" refer to in paragraph 3?

options.A: the holding of vast inventories
options.B: trade widening across oceans
options.C: just-in-time ordering of parts
options.D: digital tracking of shipments

questions.explanation: "This lean approach" points back to the just-in-time systems described at the end of paragraph 2, in which parts arrive exactly when needed rather than being stored.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about just-in-time systems?

options.A: Reducing stored inventory can increase a firm's vulnerability to disruptions.
options.B: Storing more parts always lowers a manufacturer's total costs.
options.C: Digital sensors have made supply chains completely reliable.
options.D: Ports and storms rarely affect modern shipping routes.

questions.explanation: Because the lean approach "cut costs sharply but left firms exposed when a single disruption halted the flow of parts," carrying little inventory raises the risk when supply is interrupted.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. 'For example, Japanese carmakers coordinated closely with nearby parts suppliers so that components reached the assembly line just hours before use.' Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Slot 1 [■]
options.B: Slot 2 [■]
options.C: Slot 3 [■]
options.D: Slot 4 [■]

questions.explanation: Slot 3 follows the introduction of just-in-time systems, so the example of carmakers timing part deliveries to the assembly line directly illustrates that method.

items.passage:
Plant domestication is the gradual process by which humans transformed wild plants into dependable crops through generations of selective harvesting. Early foragers faced an unreliable food supply, since wild grains scattered their seeds the moment they ripened and were hard to gather in quantity. Storing enough to survive a lean season was nearly impossible, and a single poor year could threaten an entire community.

These pressures encouraged people to favor certain plants over others. Foragers naturally collected seeds from stalks that held onto their grain longest, and by replanting them, they slowly increased the share of plants with tough, non-shattering stems. Over centuries this practice produced cereals that stayed on the stalk until harvest, yielding far larger and more predictable returns.

This steadier harvest reshaped human life. Reliable surpluses allowed populations to settle in one place, support larger families, and free some members for crafts and trade rather than constant food-gathering. As archaeologists refine their dating methods and recover more preserved seeds, the study of domestication promises an even clearer picture of how farming remade human society.
paragraphs: Plant domestication is the gradual process by which humans transformed wild plants into dependable crops through generations of selective harvesting. Early foragers faced an unreliable food supply, since wild grains scattered their seeds the moment they ripened and were hard to gather in quantity. Storing enough to survive a lean season was nearly impossible, and a single poor year could threaten an entire community.

These pressures encouraged people to favor certain plants over others. Foragers naturally collected seeds from stalks that held onto their grain longest, and by replanting them, they slowly increased the share of plants with tough, non-shattering stems. Over centuries this practice produced cereals that stayed on the stalk until harvest, yielding far larger and more predictable returns.

This steadier harvest reshaped human life. Reliable surpluses allowed populations to settle in one place, support larger families, and free some members for crafts and trade rather than constant food-gathering. As archaeologists refine their dating methods and recover more preserved seeds, the study of domestication promises an even clearer picture of how farming remade human society.

questions.stem: The word "scattered" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: buried
options.B: dispersed
options.C: stored
options.D: colored

questions.explanation: Wild grains "scattered their seeds the moment they ripened and were hard to gather," so the seeds were dispersed widely rather than kept in one place.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, how did foragers gradually change wild cereals?

options.A: By replanting seeds from stalks that held onto their grain
options.B: By watering wild fields during long dry seasons
options.C: By removing every plant that had tough stems
options.D: By trading their best seeds with neighboring groups

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 says foragers "collected seeds from stalks that held onto their grain longest, and by replanting them" increased the share of non-shattering plants.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about settled farming communities?

options.A: Farming caused the total human population to fall.
options.B: Settled groups gave up trade with other people.
options.C: Craftwork disappeared once farming began.
options.D: A stable food surplus allowed some people to specialize in non-farming work.

questions.explanation: Because reliable surpluses "free some members for crafts and trade rather than constant food-gathering," a dependable surplus made specialized, non-farming roles possible.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention that wild grains scattered their seeds the moment they ripened in paragraph 1?

options.A: To show that wild plants were remarkably easy to harvest
options.B: To argue that foraging was more efficient than early farming
options.C: To illustrate why the wild food supply was difficult and unreliable
options.D: To describe how modern cereals are gathered today

questions.explanation: The detail supports the claim that foragers "faced an unreliable food supply," explaining concretely why wild grains were hard to gather and store.

questions.stem: According to the passage, all of the following resulted from more reliable harvests EXCEPT

options.A: a decline in permanent settlements
options.B: the growth of larger families
options.C: the ability to settle in one place
options.D: the freeing of some members for crafts and trade

questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 says reliable surpluses let people "settle in one place, support larger families," and pursue crafts and trade; a decline in settlements is the opposite of what is stated.

items.passage:
Scenic design is the craft of shaping the physical space and visual background of a theatrical performance. In ancient Greek theaters, this craft was minimal: actors performed in the open air before a plain wooden structure called the skene, which served as both backdrop and dressing room. Painted panels occasionally suggested a palace or grove, but the vast outdoor space left little room for detailed illusion.

This reliance on suggestion gave way to spectacle during the Italian Renaissance. Architects enclosed the stage behind a decorative frame, the proscenium arch, and arranged painted flats in receding rows to create a convincing sense of depth. Yet these elaborate perspective settings worked only from certain seats, and their fixed vanishing point distorted the scene for spectators sitting to the sides.

These limitations pushed designers toward greater flexibility. Movable scenery, mechanized platforms, and later electric lighting let a single stage shift quickly between many locations and moods. Modern designers treat the set less as a painted picture than as a three-dimensional environment, using light, texture, and space together to shape how an audience reads a story.
paragraphs: Scenic design is the craft of shaping the physical space and visual background of a theatrical performance. In ancient Greek theaters, this craft was minimal: actors performed in the open air before a plain wooden structure called the skene, which served as both backdrop and dressing room. Painted panels occasionally suggested a palace or grove, but the vast outdoor space left little room for detailed illusion.

This reliance on suggestion gave way to spectacle during the Italian Renaissance. Architects enclosed the stage behind a decorative frame, the proscenium arch, and arranged painted flats in receding rows to create a convincing sense of depth. Yet these elaborate perspective settings worked only from certain seats, and their fixed vanishing point distorted the scene for spectators sitting to the sides.

These limitations pushed designers toward greater flexibility. Movable scenery, mechanized platforms, and later electric lighting let a single stage shift quickly between many locations and moods. Modern designers treat the set less as a painted picture than as a three-dimensional environment, using light, texture, and space together to shape how an audience reads a story.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?

options.A: The construction and daily use of the ancient Greek skene
options.B: How theatrical scenic design developed from simple backdrops toward complex modern environments
options.C: The invention of the proscenium arch in Renaissance Italy
options.D: The role of electric lighting in contemporary theater

questions.explanation: The passage traces scenic design across three eras — Greek, Renaissance, and modern — describing its overall development rather than any single stage or device.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, what purpose did the skene serve?

options.A: It displayed the theater's painted perspective flats.
options.B: It served as both a backdrop and a dressing room.
options.C: It provided tiered seating for the audience.
options.D: It housed the stage's mechanical platforms.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 describes the skene as "a plain wooden structure ... which served as both backdrop and dressing room."

questions.stem: How does paragraph 2 relate to paragraph 1?

options.A: It traces the next stage of development, from simple suggestion toward elaborate illusion.
options.B: It contradicts paragraph 1 by rejecting the use of any backdrops.
options.C: It provides statistical evidence for the claims made in paragraph 1.
options.D: It restates paragraph 1's account of Greek theater in different words.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 opens by noting that "reliance on suggestion gave way to spectacle," continuing the chronological account by moving from the sparse Greek stage to detailed Renaissance illusion.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about Renaissance perspective settings?

options.A: They were admired equally by every member of the audience.
options.B: They were far cheaper to build than Greek backdrops.
options.C: A spectator's view of the scene depended on where they were seated.
options.D: They removed the need for any painted scenery.

questions.explanation: Because the settings "worked only from certain seats" and "distorted the scene for spectators sitting to the sides," the quality of the view depended on seating position.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 3, how do modern designers treat the set?

options.A: As a plain wooden structure similar to the skene
options.B: As a fixed perspective painting seen from one point
options.C: As a decorative frame surrounding the actors
options.D: As a three-dimensional environment shaped by light, texture, and space

questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 says modern designers treat the set "less as a painted picture than as a three-dimensional environment, using light, texture, and space together."
